companyCaseStudy.title: How Discord Scaled Real‑Time Chat & Voice to Hundreds of Millions of Users
companyCaseStudy.summary: Discord grew to ~150M monthly users and had to deliver sub-100ms chat and millions of simultaneous voice connections. This case study shows how they solved the problem with BEAM/Elixir for text, Go for voice, sharded routing, stream processing, and pragmatic caching — and what measurable improvements those choices delivered.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
Discord went from a niche gamer chat app to a platform with roughly 150M monthly users and millions of concurrent real‑time connections. The engineering problem was simple to state and fiendishly hard to solve: deliver instant, ordered chat messages and low‑latency, resilient voice streams for millions of users simultaneously — while keeping servers manageable, costs predictable, and latency sub‑100ms for interactive experiences.

### Problem
- Scale: hundreds of millions of users, millions of concurrent WebSocket connections.
- Latency: chat must be delivered in tens to low hundreds of milliseconds.
- State: presence (who's online), per‑room routing, and fan‑out for servers with tens of thousands of users.
- Voice: thousands of concurrent voice channels with real‑time media (UDP/WebRTC), NAT traversal, and per‑channel mixing/relay.
- Reliability: noisy neighbors (one busy server) shouldn't take down the rest; connections are stateful and expensive to move.

### Challenges
- TCP/WebSocket connection counts explode on a single host; OS and language choices matter.
- Maintaining routing tables for presence and channel membership at global scale.
- Fan‑out: broadcast a message from one user to a channel with tens of thousands of users without saturating a single origin.
- Voice media requires UDP and often needs specialized servers for relaying/mixing, with autoscaling and transcoders.

### Architectural Decisions (high level)
- Use BEAM/Elixir for the chat/gateway layer to exploit massive concurrency and soft real‑time scheduling.
- Shard user connections across many gateway nodes; each gateway manages many WebSockets and short‑lived state.
- Use a fast, partitioned message bus (internal pub/sub + Kafka/stream system) to fan out messages reliably across datacenters.
- Store durable history/metadata in a combination of relational DBs and high‑throughput stores (caches for hot reads, long‑term stores for history).
- Implement voice stack in Go/C++ using UDP and WebRTC, with specialized voice servers that can relay, mix, or passthrough depending on channel size.
- Instrument heavily and autoscale both gateway and voice pools using real traffic signals (RTT, queue length, CPU, connection churn).

### How the pieces fit together

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Client (Web/Mobile)] -->|WebSocket/WS| EdgeLB[Edge Load Balancer]
  EdgeLB --> GW1[Gateway Node A (Elixir)]
  EdgeLB --> GW2[Gateway Node B (Elixir)]
  GW1 --> MB[Message Bus / PubSub Cluster]
  GW2 --> MB
  MB --> Store[Long-term Storage (Postgres / Sharded DB)]
  MB --> Cache[Redis / In-memory shard]
  Client -->|WebRTC/UDP| VoiceEdge[Voice Edge / TURN]
  VoiceEdge --> VoicePool1[Voice Region Pool (Go)]
  VoicePool1 --> Metrics[Monitoring / Tracing]
  MB --> VoicePool1
```

- Gateways handle connection lifecycle, presence, typing events, and immediate delivery.
- Message Bus fans messages between gateways so that a user on Gateway A can instantly reach users on Gateway B without each gateway aware of all connections.
- Redis (or in‑process caches) hold hot channel membership lists for sub‑millisecond access and to reduce bus fan‑out overhead.
- Voice edges handle NAT/TURN. Voice pools handle heavy media work; small channels may be peer‑to‑peer; large channels use relays/transcoders.

### Key implementation details
- BEAM/Elixir: chosen because the Erlang VM excels at tens of thousands of lightweight processes and fault isolation. Each WebSocket is a process; crashing a process only affects that connection.
- Sharding: users are sharded by user ID hash into gateway clusters. Shards are moved rarely; instead routing uses a consistent lookup (and sticky sessions) to avoid moving state.
- Pub/Sub: a partitioned, durable streaming system (internal pub/sub layered with Kafka or equivalent) ensures messages are delivered once to the appropriate gateway partitions; gateways subscribe only to the channels relevant to their current connections.
- Fan‑out optimization: Instead of naively publishing every message to every interested gateway, membership is partitioned and gateways maintain compact membership bloom filters or member maps. For very large channels, the system uses multicast-like patterns where only a handful of dispatcher nodes do cross‑region fan‑out.
- Caching & coalescing: presence updates are coalesced to avoid thrash (e.g., frequent ephemeral connect/disconnects), and hot lists are cached in Redis with short TTLs and versioning to ensure freshness while avoiding stampedes.
- Voice: UDP media is anchored at edge TURN servers. For small group calls, WebRTC peer connections may be preferred; for large rooms, media is forwarded to specialized relay clusters that can scale horizontally and do selective forwarding.

### Before vs After (Representative metrics)
| Metric | Before (early scale) | After (post rearchitecture) |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| Avg chat delivery latency | 250–400 ms | 20–80 ms |
| Concurrent WS per gateway | 10k–50k (OS limits) | 200k+ (BEAM processes, sharding) |
| CPU per gateway | 70–90% (unstable) | 20–40% (with autoscaling) |
| Voice call success rate under peak | 85% (dropped / failed connects) | 99.5% |
| Mean time to recover (failover) | minutes (manual) | < 30s (automated) |

### Results (numbers and outcomes)
- Latency for chat deliveries dropped from hundreds of milliseconds to tens of milliseconds for the majority of messages; interactive experiences felt instant.
- Gateway host CPU usage stabilized and hosts could support an order of magnitude more connections, reducing per‑connection cost.
- Voice reliability rose: successful call establishment and stability increased into the high 99% range and call drops decreased.
- Incident blast radius reduced by process isolation on BEAM and better autoscaling for voice clusters; outages became smaller and faster to mitigate.

### Operational patterns and reliability
- Chaos / failure testing: deliberate failure of gateway processes to validate that only affected connections drop and that reconnection logic on clients can route to other gateways.
- Aggressive monitoring: per‑gateway and per‑voice pool KPIs, tail latency tracking, and business signals (e.g., rooms per minute) used to scale ahead of demand.
- Idempotency and ordering: messages assigned monotonic sequence IDs per channel; clients reconcile using gaps to request missing messages or histories.

### Tradeoffs and why it worked
- BEAM/Elixir trades raw single‑thread throughput for massive concurrency and survivability; for millions of connections it outperforms many thread/process models.
- Using a stream/pubsub in front of storage decouples real‑time delivery from durable stores; that reduces write load on DBs and helps keep latency low.
- Splitting voice from chat (different languages/stacks) allowed independent scaling and optimization for very different workloads.

### Visual summary
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[User opens app] --> B{Join channel?}
  B -->|Yes| C[Connect to Gateway (Elixir)]
  C --> D[Subscribe to channel via Pub/Sub]
  D --> E[Receive messages from other gateways]
  C --> F{Voice required?}
  F -->|Yes| G[Signaling to Voice Edge]
  G --> H[TURN / Relay (Go)]
  H --> I[Voice Pool (media relays/mixers)]
  E --> J[Cache: Redis for hot membership]
  J --> D
```

### Lessons for engineers
- Pick the right tool for the shape of the problem (BEAM for many small concurrent tasks, Go/C++ for raw media throughput).
- Shard state and route, don't try to centralize per‑connection state.
- Cache hot membership lists aggressively and coalesce presence events.
- Separate control plane (signaling, presence) from data plane (media) so each can scale on its own.
keyTakeaways: Use the right runtime for connection density: BEAM/Elixir provided huge concurrency wins for millions of WebSockets, reducing per‑connection cost and improving stability.
Separate control (chat/presence) from data (voice/media). Different languages and architectures can coexist and scale independently — e.g., Elixir for chat, Go for voice.
Optimize fan‑out: maintain compact membership maps and coalesce events to avoid overwhelming the message bus; autoscale voice relays based on real traffic signals to keep success rates >99%.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
defmodule Gateway.SocketHandler do
  use GenServer

  # Simplified: every WebSocket connection is a lightweight process
  def start_link(socket) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, socket)
  end

  def init(socket) do
    # track presence, subscribe to channel topics, etc.
    {:ok, %{socket: socket, subscribed_topics: MapSet.new()}}
  end

  def handle_info({:pubsub, topic, msg}, state) do
    # push to the websocket (non-blocking); the BEAM scheduler keeps this cheap
    WebSocket.push(state.socket, msg)
    {:noreply, state}
  end
end

concept.title: Bloom Filters: Fast 'Probably In' Checks That Save IO and Money
concept.summary: A Bloom filter is a compact probabilistic data structure that answers "is this item present?" with no false negatives and tunable false positives. It prevents expensive lookups (disk, DB, external services) and reduces waste when exact answers aren't required for every check.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Imagine your web app gets millions of requests per minute asking "does user X exist?" or "do we have this object cached?" Each miss can cause a DB or network call that costs latency and resources. A Bloom filter gives you a cheap, in-memory *probabilistic* gate: most non-existing items are rejected quickly and inexpensively, saving expensive operations for likely positives.

**Analogy:** a Bloom filter is like a bouncer with a guest-list stamp. If your stamp pattern isn't on the guest's hand the bouncer says "definitely not invited" (no false negatives). If it *is* stamped, the bouncer says "probably invited" — sometimes a false positive slips through and you check the real list.

---

## How it works (simple explanation)

- A Bloom filter is a bit array of length m, initially all zero.
- You pick k independent hash functions. To add an item, hash it k times and set the k bit positions to 1.
- To test membership, hash the item k times; if any of the k bits is 0, the item is *definitely not present*; if all k are 1, the item is *probably present* (there's a false positive probability p).

### Mermaid: Typical request flow with a Bloom filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  R[Client Request] --> B{Bloom Filter}
  B -->|Not Present (definitely)| X[Return Not Found]
  B -->|Probably Present| C[Cache / DB Lookup]
  C --> D{Found?}
  D -->|Yes| S[Return Data]
  D -->|No (false positive)| E[Return Not Found]
```

### Sequence view (quick cache-probe + Bloom)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>API: Request
  API->>Bloom: Check(key)
  Bloom-->>API: No -> return 404
  Bloom-->>API: Yes -> API->>Cache: Get(key)
  Cache-->>API: Hit/Miss -> maybe DB lookup
```

---

## Before / After (practical comparison)

| Before (no Bloom) | After (Bloom in front) |
|-------------------|------------------------|
| Every request probes the cache or DB -> high miss traffic | Most negative requests are filtered in-memory -> fewer DB calls |
| Higher latency on misses, higher DB load | Lower average latency, lower DB load, cheaper infra |
| Simpler semantics (exact) | Slightly more complex: must handle false positives |

### Concrete before/after example (numbers)

| Metric | Before | After (Bloom with p=0.01) |
|--------|--------:|--------------------------:|
| Requests per min | 1,000,000 | 1,000,000 |
| Fraction negative lookups | 60% | 60% |
| DB calls/min (negatives) | 600,000 | 6,000 (1% false positive of negatives)
| Memory for Bloom | 0 MB | 16 MB (tunable) |

---

## Math: choose size m and number of hashes k

For n expected items and desired false-positive rate p:

- m = - (n * ln p) / (ln 2)^2
- k = (m / n) * ln 2

Example: n = 1,000,000, p = 0.01 -> m ≈ 9.6 million bits (~1.2 MB), k ≈ 7

---

## Code example (Python): tiny Bloom filter

```python
# Simple Bloom filter using hashlib and Python's int as bit array
import hashlib
import math

class BloomFilter:
    def __init__(self, n, p):
        # n = expected items, p = false positive rate
        m = - (n * math.log(p)) / (math.log(2) ** 2)
        self.m = int(m)
        self.k = max(1, int((self.m / n) * math.log(2)))
        self.bits = 0  # using Python int as a bitset for simplicity

    def _hashes(self, item):
        b = item.encode('utf-8')
        h1 = int(hashlib.md5(b).hexdigest(), 16)
        h2 = int(hashlib.sha1(b).hexdigest(), 16)
        for i in range(self.k):
            yield (h1 + i * h2) % self.m

    def add(self, item):
        for pos in self._hashes(item):
            self.bits |= (1 << pos)

    def __contains__(self, item):
        return all((self.bits >> pos) & 1 for pos in self._hashes(item))

# Usage
bf = BloomFilter(n=1000000, p=0.01)
bf.add('alice@example.com')
if 'bob@example.com' in bf:
    print('probably present')
else:
    print('definitely not')
```

Notes: in production, use bit arrays (bitarray, Redis bitmap) and high-quality hash functions (MurmurHash3 or xxHash). This code shows the concept.

---

## When to use a Bloom filter

- You have a **high rate of reads** and expensive misses (disk/DB/external). 
- Memory is limited and you need a compact representation.
- You can tolerate **false positives** but **not false negatives**.
- Examples: preventing cache penetration (filter out non-existent keys), indexing large data sets before disk lookup (Bigtable used Bloom filters per SSTable), spam/pre-filtering, set membership in distributed systems.

## Variants and alternatives

- **Counting Bloom Filter**: supports removals by storing small counters instead of bits (cost: more memory).
- **Cuckoo Filter**: lower false positive rates for the same memory and supports deletion; often faster lookups in practice.
- **Blocked/Partitioned Bloom Filters**: improve cache locality by restricting hashes to a block.

---

## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- **Wrong sizing**: underestimate n and your false-positive rate skyrockets. Provision for growth or rebuild strategy.
- **Hash collisions & poor hashes**: use well-distributed, fast hashes (Murmur/xxHash) and combine them properly.
- **No deletions**: standard Bloom filters can't delete; if you need deletes, use counting or cuckoo filters.
- **Operational complexity**: when deploying in front of caches/DBs, false positives cause rare extra lookups — account for that in SLA planning.
- **Synchronization**: in distributed systems, keep Bloom filters in sync or accept slightly stale filters depending on your use case. Stale filters cause increased false positives or missed entries depending on update timing.

---

## Deployment tips

- Store Bloom filters in memory (on each app node) for lowest latency, or in a shared in-memory store (Redis) if you need a single source of truth.
- Rebuild filters offline and swap atomically if you need to add many items.
- Monitor the actual false positive rate by sampling checks; if it drifts, rebuild with updated n.

---

## Summary

A Bloom filter is a tiny probabilistic sentinel that can eliminate most expensive misses before they cost you CPU, latency, or dollars. Use it when you want a fast, memory-efficient "probably in" gate and can tolerate a controlled rate of false positives. When you need deletions or zero false positives, look at counting or cuckoo filters instead.
keyTakeaways: Bloom filters provide compact, fast membership checks with no false negatives and tunable false positives.
Tune m (bits) and k (#hashes) using m = -n ln p / (ln 2)^2 and k = (m/n) ln 2; underestimate n and p will balloon.
Use variants (counting / cuckoo) when you need deletions or lower false positives; monitor and rebuild as data grows.
